While the deal gives the company added growth potential, it also gave the market some added concerns about the cost of the deal and the debt being managed by H2O America. Its stock has declined about 10% since the proposed acquisition was announced.

If approved by regulators, the transaction should close in the second or third quarter of 2026. It would give added diversity to HTO’s operations which are currently heavily reliant on operations in California. By 2029, Texas could represent just over a quarter of the company’s business activity. Today that number is about 7%. H2O America has previously stated that it plans to spend more than $500 million in the Lone Star State in capital improvements over the next five years. As a result of the Quadvest acquisition, the company has raised its five-year capital spending plan about 6% to reach a total of $2.1 billion.

Just one month after the Quadvest announcement, HTO also began the process of purchasing the Cibolo Valley wastewater treatment plant. According to the press release from H2O America, the acquisition of the Cibolo Valley assets will expand the company’s service footprint in the Texas Hill Country in Central Texas, more than doubling its current existing customer base of approximately 1,200 connections. The Cibolo Valley wastewater system also has an additional 250 wastewater connections “under contract and pending construction”. This transaction should officially close in late 2026.

Risks to Consider

There are a few risks to consider before investing in H2O America. The first is the chance that interest rates will stay higher than expected for a longer period of time. Dividend stocks like HTO are often more attractive during periods of lower rates as their yield becomes a substitute for other options for low-risk income.

Higher rates could also impact H2O America’s own situation with debt, which would come at a time when its credit outlook is under some pressure. On July 15, S&P Global updated the outlook for the company to negative based on the possibility that its ratio of consolidated funds from operations to debt could fall below the downgrade threshold of 11%. As part of its report, S&P Global made the following statement with regards to its projections:

Although HTO has solid market access, its exceptionally large short- to medium-term funding plan raises its execution risk. The company plans to fund the $540 million transaction with a mix of common equity and long-term debt. We believe the acquisition will be incremental to its robust $2 billion capital spending program for 2025-2029. Although HTO has consistently raised its common equity on an annual basis since 2021--including through its $200 million at-the-market program--the Quadvest acquisition would be HTO’s largest so far. Therefore, we believe the company will face execution risk over the short- to medium-term. An overreliance on debt leverage would further contribute to a potential weakening in its financial measures.

Another risk to consider is the ever-constant struggle the company must endure with state regulators to approve rate increases. This is nothing new for regulated utilities. Below is a list of key rate requests that have been filed by H2O America in the last couple of years. The company has been approved for the full amount of their requested revenue in 13 out of 16 cases, which is an impressive statistic, but there are no guarantees that future requests will have the same rate of success.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating currently gives HTO a Sell rating, despite its score of “A-” in Valuation. The stock gets a failing mark in Momentum, which is certainly well-deserved after its performance over the last year, a time when the stock price has dropped 16%. HTO receives a “D-” in Profitability even though the company receives all A’s and B’s in gross profit margin, EBITDA margin, and net income margin. The grade is hampered by a D related to issues surrounding the company’s free cash flow.

The Bottom Line

I never expected HTO to take off like a tech stock, but of course, I did not expect it to drop by almost 10% in five months either. After my most recent review of the company’s operations, I do see a little more risk here than initially thought, and that may justify some of the market’s pessimism with stock. However, I do think much of the focus for utility stocks is centered around power producers and their link to AI data centers, an area where HTO loses much of its appeal.

On a historical basis, H2O America looks like a relative bargain at its current price. The stock has a PE ratio of 14.8 when measured against its earnings for the last 12 months. This is the lowest valuation for the stock since 2016. A graph showing the historical trailing PE valuation for HTO since 2012 is shown below.

As we head into what is likely to be a volatile new year, the inelastic demand for water that creates consistent returns combined with the stock's current valuation and growing dividends keeps me on the Buy side of things for HTO.

data.title: 2026 S&P 500 Outlook: My Target Is 10,000 Due To The Devaluation Of Fiat Currencies
data.description:
Markets are ignoring a critical event in a case of widespread cognitive dissonance- The destruction of fiat currencies.

Yogi Berra was a Hall of Fame Yankee catcher and armchair philosopher who once said that “The future ain’t what it used to be.” Yogi’s common-sense observation, where he also said, “You can observe a lot by watching,” is the reason why I believe that the most diversified U.S. stock market index, the S&P 500 (SP500), will trade at 10,000 in 2026.

On December 4, the index is at 6,850, which suggests an over 45% rally from the current price level in the coming year. It is not that I am so bullish on stocks; instead, the value of money, or fiat currency, has declined and will continue to deteriorate over the coming months and years. Moreover, historically high P/E ratios do not matter in the current environment, as a significant indicator is screaming that money’s purchasing power has fallen off a cliff, making the equities in the S&P 500 index downright cheap.

The S&P 500’s trend is higher

Over the past ten years, the S&P 500 has posted substantial gains in 8 years:

  • 2016: +9.54%
  • 2017: +19.42%
  • 2018: -6.24%
  • 2019: +28.88%
  • 2020: +16.26%
  • 2021: +26.89%
  • 2022: -19.44%
  • 2023: +24.23%
  • 2024: +23.31%.

The S&P 500 closed 2024 at 5,881.63. On December 3 at 6,849.72, the index is 16.5% higher.

The quarterly chart shows that the leading U.S. stock market index posted gains in eight of the past ten years.

2025 was a unique year- Gold and silver, the world’s oldest currencies, reached record highs in a parabolic rally

In 1999, the Bank of England liquidated half of the U.K.’s gold holdings, sending gold (XAUUSD:CUR) to a significant low of $252.80 per ounce.

Over the past 10 years, gold has posted gains in 8 years:

  • 2016: +8.66%
  • 2017: +13.65%
  • 2018: -2.14%
  • 2019: +3.92%
  • 2020: +24.42%
  • 2021: +3.51%
  • 2022: -0.13%
  • 2023: +13.45%
  • 2024: +27.47%.

Gold closed 2024 at $2,641. On December 3, the precious metal is 60.4% higher at $4,236.80 in 2025.

The ascent of the precious metals is a commentary on fiat money’s purchasing power

Gold is a commodity, but it is much more as it is the world’s oldest means of exchange and a store of value. Value’s definition is the amount of money that something is worth, an equivalent in goods, services, or money for something exchanged, the relative worth, utility, or importance, something that can be purchased for a fair price, something intrinsically valuable or desirable, and a numerical quantity that is assigned or is determined by calculation or measurement.

Over the past years, central banks have been loading up on gold, making it one of the leading reserve assets.

Source: visualcapitalist.com.

Central banks will purchase at least another 900 tons, adding to reserves in 2025, according to JP Morgan. Moreover, reserves are likely understated as China and Russia are the leading gold producers and may be vacuuming up domestic production at levels far above official data. Gold is a strategic economic asset, reserves are a national security matter, and they are state secrets in China and Russia.

The bottom line is that gold’s role, validated by the world’s central banks and governments, has grown to regain its historical position as the ultimate currency asset. Gold’s ascent is more than a commentary on fiat currency values, as the precious metal has appreciated versus all of them. Gold’s price suggests that fiat currencies’ purchasing power has declined at an accelerated pace, with fascinating implications for the value of companies in the S&P 500 index.

The S&P 500 in gold terms- The leading stock market index has a lot of catching up on the horizon

This ten-year monthly chart highlights reflect the S&P 500 index (SPX) divided by the price of nearby continuous COMEX gold futures. Interestingly, the SPX in gold terms has fallen 15.6% from the start of 2016 through December 3, 2025. The average over the past 10 years has been around 2 SPX units for each ounce of gold. The bottom line is that stocks are cheap.

10,000 is a logical target as the future ain’t what it used to be

A November 28, 2025, CNBC article quoted a Goldman Sachs survey of 900 institutional investor clients, in which 36% of respondents, a plurality, “expect gold to maintain its momentum and exceed $5,000 per troy ounce by the end of next year.” In this case, a return to the average relationship between the precious metal and the leading U.S. benchmark equity index at 2:1 would put the S&P 500 at 10,000.

Gold is telling us that the benchmark pricing mechanism for the U.S. stock market and the S&P 500 is flawed, making U.S. stocks inexpensive at current levels. Thus, my call is that the S&P 500 will ignore P/E ratios and other traditional metrics and will rise to my 10,000 target sometime in 2026. The SPX at 10,000 would have a significant bullish impact on all U.S. and other stock market indices.

When it comes to fiat currency values, Yogi Berra’s quote from years ago may very well turn out to be prescient, as the future ain’t what it used to be.

Any investment involves substantial risks, including, but not limited to, pricing volatility, inadequate liquidity, and the potential complete loss of principal. This document does not in any way constitute an offer or solicitation of an offer to buy or sell any investment, security, or commodity discussed herein, or any security in any jurisdiction in which such an offer would be unlawful under the securities laws of such jurisdiction.

data.title: Willdan Group: Why I'm Buying This Overlooked Grid Consultant
data.description:
Intro & Thesis

Wildan Group (WLDN) is a small-cap engineering consulting firm that works with power companies and large data centers, advising them on how to make their energy systems smarter and more reliable. They also advise local governments on how to plan and finance long‑term infrastructure and energy projects.

The WLDN stock was one of the most beaten-down Industrial stocks from 2021 to 2023, when its price dropped by almost 80%. But then the setup quickly changed, and WLDN rose from the ashes like a phoenix:

Right now, WLDN trades 17-18% off its 52-week high, and I think that, despite seemingly high valuation multiples (as of the time of this writing), the stock looks tempting for investors looking for exposure to Industrial names within the U.S. They're organically expanding at double digits, still having a reasonable FCF yield despite a high P/E ratio. What's more important for growth/GARP investors is that there's a structurally high demand for what WLDN does best. So, I think that the current dip presents a buying opportunity, so I decided to rate WLDN as a "Buy".

Why Do I Think So?

Wildan reported for its fiscal Q3 2025 in late November, showing $182.01 million in sales (+15% YoY) and $1.21 in adjusted EPS (+66% YoY), beating the consensus estimates by 12.14% and 57.89%, respectively. Interestingly, the double-beating hasn't led to any changes in analysts' views on the Q4, which I explain by the existing lack of investment coverage.

Anyway, speaking of net revenues for the quarter, the organic growth was ~20% YoY (the rest came from WLDN's M&A activity). On a YTD basis, the organic growth was at +21% YoY, and the firm boosted its EBITDA margin to 21.6% (the absolute figure went up by 52% YoY). The FCF soared to $34 million for the past 9 months, and it's trailing at ~$65 million on a TTM basis. So, that's effectively a FCF yield of ~4.35% if my math is correct, which is great for any industrial company these days. What I like is that their total debt decreased by over 37% since FY2024, and so their organic expansion is basically de-leveraged at its core.

What makes the current business expansion look compelling is the momentum in the end markets, which seems structural. Based on the latest earnings call, Willdan's subsidiary (E3) expects 0.7-1.2 terawatt‑hours of incremental U.S. electricity load growth over the next 10 years. The IR PowerPoint presentation shows that there should be a 50% increase in U.S. electricity demand over the next 25 years or so:

The demand is mainly driven by AI, clouds, DCs, and onshoring of advanced manufacturing in the U.S., while EVs and the ongoing HVAC conversion only add to the demand velocity. As AI moves from hype to real‑world infrastructure constraints, Wildan's business starts to benefit because someone has to plan, design, and deliver the extra power and efficiency that such systems need.

A lot of utility companies, DCs, and even local government agencies will have to turn to specialists like Willdan to figure out where the grid is weak and how to upgrade it or how to cut waste so they can free up capacity. Thus, their need will translate to more long‑term contracts for engineering work, and Wildan's organic growth is very likely to stay stably superior to what it used to be before.

Wildan has beaten every single quarter for the past 2 years, and the management has already raised its guidance twice in 2025 (after Q2 and Q3), but the market isn't appreciating that at all. At least the EPS revisions are negative despite upward revenue revisions, which suggests to me that analysts are not bullish on WLDN's margins going forward.

Earlier this year, Wildan bought APG, an electrical power engineering and construction management specialist that generated ~$34 million in sales in FY2024. While it doesn't look like a major addition in terms of the asset base, APG is actually leading multiple new substation and grid projects across Texas, Oregon, Georgia, and Utah, with tens of millions in awards (just since the last call), and the management expects 50%+ growth at it next year on the back of record backlog.

Our upfront policy, forecasting and data analytics work informs our strategy and helps us navigate market change. In our upfront work, we see particular demand for studies on the impacts of electricity load growth, and that work is growing at about 50% organically year-over-year.

Those market changes led us to the APG acquisition that provides Power Engineering solutions to data center clients, hyperscalers and other commercial customers. I'm pleased to report that APG is collaborating very effectively with the rest of Willdan and has already won record backlog that we expect will propel more than 50% growth by APG in 2026.

Source: WLDN's Q3 earnings call

Given that APG's work is currently tightly integrated with WLDN's E3 analytics, it tends to have relatively high margins (compared to generic EPS work). Assuming that APG's backlog converts to revenue relatively fast, or if additional large‑ticket DC interconnect work is signed, the consensus estimates for both revenue and margin in 2026 will likely be revised upward, which sounds like a bullish catalyst to me.

WLDN's smart M&A strategy keeps rolling with another addition: A few days ago, the company announced that its financial services division will acquire Compass Municipal Advisors (expected closing on 01/01/2026), which is supposed to deepen Willdan's municipal finance advisory capabilities.

I assume that WLDN's actual FY2026 EPS is going to beat the current consensus estimate by 10-20%, given the lack of confidence from the Street's analysts (I judge solely based on the latest revisions). If WLDN keeps its current P/E ratio of 35x in place, and it's possible in case the firm doesn't erode its margins with all the M&A deals in place, the resulting price target of $135.24/share will give us an upside of 35-36%.

3.36*1.15*35 = $135.24/share

Source: The author's calculations

Where Can I Be Wrong?

If the core demand growth that I'm referring to throughout the article turns out to be smaller or lumpier than E3's load growth scenarios imply, the resulting organic growth rates at Wildan Group will shrink quickly. The lack of organic growth will naturally lead to stagnation, and so the company will likely lose its pricing power. The valuation multiple that I assigned above may be too high. Assuming that WLDN is valued at 20x earnings next year, even if it beats the consensus by the same 15%, its price target would drop to $77.28/share, which is a downside of over 22% to the current stock price.

Another risk is execution and integration. One of the reasons for Wildan's massive expansion in recent quarters is its successful M&As. But if they keep growing through acquisitions, it will require tighter control over project selection, staffing, risk management, etc. As a result, the execution risk is going to be high for some time.

The Bottom Line

Despite the risks to my thesis, I think WLDN stock is a "Buy" at its 17-18% off the 52-week high because it's delivering exactly what I want to see at this phase of the story. They have 20%+ organic growth, rising EBITDA margins (above 20%), consistent beats on both the top and bottom line, and strong FCF with a steadily de‑risked balance sheet. That's why the stock is valued so generously by the market, and I don't think the above metrics should deteriorate significantly; hence, I don't expect to see a much lower P/E next year. My valuation modeling says there's an upside of 35-36% if the current business momentum stays in, and that's why I'm bullish on WLDN in the medium term.

Thank you for reading!
